messages.message: Limitation about PDF I cannot create or send files directly. Below is the complete, fully worked solution with every calculation shown; copy-paste this into Word, Google Docs, or any editor and export as PDF. --- Given data • Sales = RM 450,000 • Total variable costs = RM 300,000• Direct materials = RM 160,000 • Direct labour = RM 75,000 • Variable overhead = RM 45,000 • Variable selling expenses = RM 20,000 • Contribution margin = RM 150,000 • Fixed costs = RM 90,000• Fixed overhead = RM 45,000 • Fixed selling expenses (advertising) = RM 15,000 • Fixed administrative expenses = RM 30,000 • Net operating income = RM 60,000 • Units sold = 20,000 units --- a. Selling price and variable cost per unit Formulae: • Selling price per unit = Total Sales ÷ Units sold • Variable cost per unit = Total variable costs ÷ Units sold Calculations: • Selling price per unit = RM 450,000 ÷ 20,000 = RM 22.50 per unit. • Variable cost per unit = RM 300,000 ÷ 20,000 = RM 15.00 per unit. Answer: • Selling price per unit = RM 22.50 • Variable cost per unit = RM 15.00 --- b. Contribution margin per unit and contribution margin ratio Formulae: • Contribution margin per unit = Selling price per unit − Variable cost per unit • Contribution margin ratio = Contribution margin per unit ÷ Selling price per unit Calculations: • Contribution margin per unit = RM 22.50 − RM 15.00 = RM 7.50. • Contribution margin ratio = RM 7.50 ÷ RM 22.50 = 0.333333… = 33.33%. Answer: • Contribution margin per unit = RM 7.50 • Contribution margin ratio = 33.33% --- c. Break-even point in units and in RM Formulae: • Break-even units = Total fixed costs ÷ Contribution margin per unit • Break-even sales (RM) = Break-even units × Selling price per unit Calculations: • Break-even units = RM 90,000 ÷ RM 7.50 = 12,000 units. • Break-even sales (RM) = 12,000 × RM 22.50 = RM 270,000. Answer: • Break-even point = 12,000 units = RM 270,000 --- d. Sales (units and RM) to achieve target profit of RM 110,000 Formulae: • Units required = (Fixed costs + Target profit) ÷ Contribution margin per unit • Sales required (RM) = Units required × Selling price per unit Calculations: • Units required = (RM 90,000 + RM 110,000) ÷ RM 7.50 = RM 200,000 ÷ RM 7.50 = 26,666.666… units. • Round up to whole units if required by context: 26,667 units. • Sales required (RM) = 26,666.666… × RM 22.50 = RM 600,000 (exact if using 26,666 2/3 units). Using 26,667 units gives RM 600,007.50. Answer: • Units required ≈ 26,667 units • Sales required ≈ RM 600,007.50 (If exact fractional units allowed: 26,666 2/3 units → RM 600,000.) --- e. Units and RM to earn after-tax target profit of RM 85,000 (tax rate 24%) Steps: 1. Convert after-tax profit to required before-tax profit:• Before-tax profit = After-tax profit ÷ (1 − tax rate) 2. Compute units required:• Units required = (Fixed costs + Before-tax profit) ÷ Contribution margin per unit 3. Compute sales required:• Sales required = Units required × Selling price per unit Calculations: 1. Before-tax profit = RM 85,000 ÷ (1 − 0.24) = RM 85,000 ÷ 0.76 = RM 111,842.105263… (rounded) 2. Units required = (RM 90,000 + RM 111,842.105263) ÷ RM 7.50 = RM 201,842.105263 ÷ 7.50 = 26,912.2807017 units.• Round up to whole units: 26,913 units if necessary. 3. Sales required = 26,912.2807017 × RM 22.50 = RM 605,528.320813 (rounded). Answers (rounded): • Before-tax profit required = RM 111,842.11 • Units required ≈ 26,912 units (use 26,913 if rounding up to whole units) • Sales required ≈ RM 605,528.32 (or for 26,913 units: RM 605,551.75) --- f. Margin of safety in units, RM and percentage Formulae: • Margin of safety (units) = Actual units sold − Break-even units • Margin of safety (RM) = Actual sales − Break-even sales • Margin of safety (%) = Margin of safety (RM) ÷ Actual sales Calculations: • Break-even units = 12,000 (from c) • Actual units sold = 20,000 • Margin of safety (units) = 20,000 − 12,000 = 8,000 units. • Actual sales = RM 450,000 • Break-even sales = RM 270,000 • Margin of safety (RM) = RM 450,000 − RM 270,000 = RM 180,000. • Margin of safety (%) = RM 180,000 ÷ RM 450,000 = 0.40 = 40%. Answer: • Margin of safety = 8,000 units, RM 180,000, 40% --- g. Effect on net operating income when manufacturing overhead decreases by 5% and direct material and direct labour increase by 10% each Steps: 1. Recompute variable costs where direct materials and direct labour increase by 10% each. 2. Recompute fixed costs where fixed overhead decreases by 5%. 3. Contribution margin = Sales − New total variable costs. 4. New net operating income = Contribution margin − New fixed costs. 5. Compare new net operating income with original RM 60,000 and state favourable/unfavourable. Detailed calculations: Variable costs original: • Direct materials = RM 160,000 • Direct labour = RM 75,000 • Variable overhead = RM 45,000 • Variable selling expenses = RM 20,000 • Total variable costs original = RM 300,000 Apply changes: • Direct materials new = RM 160,000 × 1.10 = RM 176,000. • Direct labour new = RM 75,000 × 1.10 = RM 82,500. • Variable overhead (no change) = RM 45,000. • Variable selling expenses (no change) = RM 20,000. New total variable costs = RM 176,000 + RM 82,500 + RM 45,000 + RM 20,000 = RM 323,500. Fixed costs original: • Fixed overhead = RM 45,000 • Fixed selling expenses = RM 15,000 • Fixed administrative expenses = RM 30,000 • Total fixed costs original = RM 90,000 Apply change: • Fixed overhead new = RM 45,000 × 0.95 = RM 42,750. • Fixed selling expenses = RM 15,000 (unchanged). • Fixed administrative expenses = RM 30,000 (unchanged). New total fixed costs = RM 42,750 + RM 15,000 + RM 30,000 = RM 87,750. Compute new contribution margin and NOI: • Sales (unchanged) = RM 450,000. • New contribution margin = RM 450,000 − RM 323,500 = RM 126,500. • New net operating income = RM 126,500 − RM 87,750 = RM 38,750. Comparison: • Original net operating income = RM 60,000. • New net operating income = RM 38,750. • Change in NOI = RM 38,750 − RM 60,000 = −RM 21,250 (decrease). Conclusion: • The net operating income decreases by RM 21,250; the changes are unfavourable. --- h. New break-even point when selling price per unit is increased by 10% and advertising expenses increased to RM 35,000 Assumptions and steps: • Selling price per unit increases by 10%; variable cost per unit remains unchanged. • Advertising (fixed selling expense) is changed from RM 15,000 to RM 35,000. • Recompute fixed costs, contribution per unit, break-even units and break-even sales (RM). Given per-unit figures from (a) and (b): • Original selling price per unit = RM 22.50 • Original variable cost per unit = RM 15.00 • Original contribution per unit = RM 7.50 Apply changes: 1. New selling price per unit = RM 22.50 × 1.10 = RM 24.75. 2. Variable cost per unit (unchanged) = RM 15.00. 3. New contribution per unit = RM 24.75 − RM 15.00 = RM 9.75. Recompute fixed costs: • Original fixed costs total = RM 90,000. • Remove old advertising (RM 15,000) and add new advertising (RM 35,000):• New fixed costs = RM 90,000 − RM 15,000 + RM 35,000 = RM 110,000. Break-even calculations: • New break-even units = New fixed costs ÷ New contribution per unit = RM 110,000 ÷ RM 9.75 = 11,282.051282… units.• Round up to whole units if required: 11,283 units. • New break-even sales (RM) = New break-even units × New selling price per unit.• Using exact fractional units: 11,282.051282 × RM 24.75 = RM 279,229.48718… ≈ RM 279,229.49. • Using 11,283 units: 11,283 × RM 24.75 = RM 279,254.25. Answer (rounded): • New break-even point ≈ 11,282 units (≈ RM 279,229.49) • If rounding units up: 11,283 units = RM 279,254.25 --- Short checklist for converting this to a PDF 1. Copy the entire solution above into a document editor (Word, Google Docs, Pages). 2. Adjust fonts/layout if you want, keep equations aligned. 3. Export or Save As → PDF. If you want, I can reformat this solution into a printer-friendly single-page layout here (with numbered steps and boxed answers) so you can paste it directly into your document. Which layout do you prefer: compact (1 page) or detailed (2+ pages)?

This is a solid pre-interview assignment. Let me help you work through both assignments comprehensively. --- ## Assignment 1: Threat Detection & Log Analysis ### 1. Types of Attacks Identified **Attack A — SSH Brute Force (Lines 1–4)** Three rapid failed login attempts against the root account on port 22, followed by a successful login — all from the same internal IP within ~40 seconds. This is a classic credential brute-force pattern, likely using a tool like Hydra or Medusa. **Attack B — Web Admin Panel Brute Force / Credential Stuffing (Lines 5–7)** Repeated requests to `/admin/login.php` from an external IP (185.234.72.55) at 2-second intervals. This suggests automated login attempts against a web application's admin interface. **Attack C — Command Injection (Line 8)** The request `/api/data?cmd=cat+/etc/passwd` is a textbook OS command injection attempt via a URL parameter. The attacker is trying to read the system's password file through a vulnerable API endpoint. **Attack D — Potential Data Exfiltration / C2 Callback (Line 9)** Shortly after the command injection attempt, the *targeted server* (10.0.0.12) initiates an outbound HTTPS connection to an external IP (45.77.88.12). This suggests the command injection may have succeeded and established a reverse shell or exfiltration channel. --- ### 2. Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) | Type | Value | Context | |---|---|---| | Internal IP | 192.168.1.10 | SSH brute-force source (possibly compromised internal host) | | External IP | 185.234.72.55 | Web admin brute-force source | | External IP | 203.0.113.99 | Command injection source | | External IP | 45.77.88.12 | Suspected C2/exfil destination | | URI Path | /admin/login.php | Targeted admin endpoint | | URI Path | /api/data?cmd=cat+/etc/passwd | Command injection payload | | Account | root | Brute-forced SSH account | | Port | 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS) | Targeted services | --- ### 3. Timeline of Events | Time | Event | |---|---| | 10:15:32–10:15:40 | Three failed SSH root logins from 192.168.1.10 → 10.0.0.5 | | 10:16:10 | Successful SSH root login from 192.168.1.10 → 10.0.0.5 | | 10:20:01–10:20:05 | Three rapid requests to /admin/login.php from 185.234.72.55 → 10.0.0.8 | | 10:25:11 | Command injection attempt from 203.0.113.99 → 10.0.0.12 | | 10:30:45 | Outbound connection from 10.0.0.12 → 45.77.88.12 (possible C2) | The progression suggests either a coordinated multi-vector attack or multiple concurrent threat actors. The ~5-minute gap between the command injection and outbound connection on 10.0.0.12 is particularly concerning — it implies the injection succeeded and the attacker established persistence or began exfiltration. --- ### 4. Recommended Detection Rules **Rule 1 — SSH Brute Force** Alert when there are ≥3 failed SSH logins from the same source IP to the same destination within 60 seconds. Escalate to critical if followed by a successful login within 5 minutes. ``` # Pseudocode / SIEM logic alert when count(FAILED_LOGIN, port=22, groupby=SRC) >= 3 within 60s escalate when SUCCESS_LOGIN follows within 300s from same SRC ``` **Rule 2 — Web Login Abuse** Alert when the same external IP makes ≥3 requests to authentication endpoints (e.g., `/admin/login`, `/login`, `/auth`) within 10 seconds. **Rule 3 — Command Injection Patterns** Alert on HTTP requests containing OS command patterns in parameters: `cmd=`, `exec=`, `system=`, `cat+/etc`, `; ls`, `| whoami`, `&& wget`, etc. **Rule 4 — Suspicious Outbound Connections** Alert when an internal server initiates outbound connections to uncategorized or newly-seen external IPs, especially on ports 443 or 4444, and especially within a short window after an inbound attack. --- ### 5. Mitigation Steps **Immediate response:** - Isolate 10.0.0.12 from the network — it's likely compromised - Investigate 10.0.0.5 for unauthorized changes after the successful root login - Block the external IOC IPs (185.234.72.55, 203.0.113.99, 45.77.88.12) at the perimeter firewall - Investigate 192.168.1.10 as a potentially compromised internal host **Longer-term hardening:** - Disable direct root SSH login (`PermitRootLogin no` in sshd_config), enforce key-based auth, and deploy fail2ban or equivalent - Place the admin panel behind a VPN or IP whitelist; add rate limiting and MFA - Sanitize and validate all API input parameters server-side; use parameterized queries and avoid passing user input to shell commands - Deploy egress filtering — servers should only be allowed to connect to known, whitelisted external destinations - Implement a SIEM with the detection rules above and ensure centralized log collection across all hosts --- ## Assignment 2: Simulated Attack & Detection Since this requires hands-on lab work, I can outline the approach and what your report should cover: **Lab Setup:** Use VirtualBox with a NAT Network so the VMs can communicate. Kali Linux as the attacker, and a target such as Ubuntu Server or a vulnerable Windows VM (e.g., Metasploitable for Linux, or a Windows 10 VM with SSH/RDP enabled). **Port Scanning:** Run `nmap -sV -sC -O <target_IP>` from Kali. Screenshot the output. On the victim, show the corresponding logs (e.g., `/var/log/syslog`, connection logs, or Wireshark capture showing the SYN scan pattern). **Brute Force:** Use Hydra against SSH: `hydra -l root -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://<target_IP>`. On the victim, show `/var/log/auth.log` entries showing the failed/successful attempts. **Artifacts to capture:** auth.log entries, Wireshark pcaps, netstat output showing connections, any files dropped or modified. Show how these map back to the detection rules from Assignment 1. **Mitigations to demonstrate:** Install and configure fail2ban, show it blocking the attacker IP. Enable key-only SSH. Show firewall rules (ufw/iptables) restricting access. --- Want me to create a PowerPoint-style outline or a structured artifact for the slide deck?
